metadata.title: Pong: Why the Simplest Game Is the Hardest to Explain
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Pong: Why the Simplest Game Is the Harde
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Pong is head-to-head competition shown from a top-down point of view with a deliberately minimal rule set. Players move their paddles only up and down, never left or right, which shapes every strategic possibility. There is no perfect defense: the fixed laws guarantee a returned ball can always eventually be angled past you. The goal is simple, reaching a score of eleven before your opponent, yet the constrained system stays hard to fully explain.

artifactLines: Pong is head-to-head competition shown from a top-down point of view with a deliberately minimal rule set.
Players move their paddles only up and down, never left or right, which shapes every strategic possibility.
There is no perfect defense: the fixed laws guarantee a returned ball can always eventually be angled past you.
The goal is simple, reaching a score of eleven before your opponent, yet the constrained system stays hard to fully explain.

beats.narration_text: What grabbed me is the claim that Pong is hard to explain precisely because its rules are so few: top-down view, paddles moving only up and down, no perfect defense, first to eleven wins. I want to see how that minimalism creates depth. Can you (1) write out Pong's complete rule set as formal constraints, (2) show mathematically why 'no perfect defense' follows from the paddle's limited movement and ball angle, and (3) identify the single rule I could change to break the game's balance?
